cataloging_subject.label: Generides (couplet version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English couplet version of the romance Generides: a 10,086-line poem surviving continuously in Morgan Library & Museum MS M.876, ff. 103r-152v. It excludes the independent rhyme-royal or stanzaic version, its manuscripts and early printed fragments, the adjacent works and physical codex as a whole, Dalziel's editorial working transcript, Furnivall's 1865/1866 edition and paratexts, the U-M electronic encoding, the repository XML, and digital reproductions as distinct layers. The prose manuscript summary copied before the poem and Furnivall's modern marginal explanations are represented in CME00043 but are treated as witness and edition paratext rather than changing the romance's verse form.

catalog_summary.title: Generides (couplet version)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed in the late fourteenth century; attested in a circa 1440-1450 manuscript; first printed in 1865

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus from modern version-level bibliography

assertion.notes: The parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this text from the independent stanzaic version without treating either manuscript or edition title as the abstract work's only name.
This conventional title can refer broadly to both long Middle English versions; use the preferred qualified title when version precision matters.

titles.value: A Royal Historie of the Excellent Knight Generides

scope.description: Bibliographic title of the immediate edition and a conventional long title for its couplet text

assertion.notes: The complete edition also contains an editor's preface, fragments of the stanzaic version, glossary, index, and a later postscript; those are not part of the cataloged romance.

titles.value: A man that hath litel to doone, Werk he may make him soone
Eternallie there to be! Seith Amen al, for charitie.
Here endeth the noble historie of the excellent Prince Generides
titles.supplied_by: Morgan MS M.876 colophon, with damaged words supplied in Furnivall's edition

assertion.notes: The full colophon continues with Generides's imperial and hereditary titles; bracketed words in the edition reflect manuscript damage.

agents.name: Frederick James Furnivall
notes: Editor and checker of the immediate Roxburghe Club source edition; not the medieval author.

agents.name: W. A. Dalziel
notes: Named by initials and surname in Furnivall's preface as the person who made the careful working transcript from the manuscript.

assertion.notes: No named medieval author is established. Furnivall, Dalziel, Gibbs, and Tollemache belong to later editorial, transcription, presentation, or ownership layers.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translator or adapter

assertion.notes: The prologue reports French verse rendered into Latin at Hertford before the English tale, and older cataloging treats the two English versions as independent translations from French. No exact French or Latin source text or physical exemplar has been identified, so translation is not modeled as a fully resolved direct-source chain.

scope.description: Immediate 1865 printed edition of the couplet version

notes: Furnivall established the printed reading text, editorial expansions, rubrication signals, bracketed supplies, notes, glossary, and index, and checked Dalziel's transcript and the printed text.

scope.description: Working transcript of the couplet text used for Furnivall's edition

notes: The working transcript is a material intermediary in the edition's lineage, not the medieval work itself.

assertion.notes: This is a work-level scholarly date, not the 1440-1450 production date of M.876.

date_statements.display: Attested in Morgan MS M.876, circa 1440-1450

assertion.notes: This is the sole known continuous manuscript of the couplet version.

date_statements.display: First printed in Furnivall's Roxburghe Club edition, 1865

assertion.notes: Furnivall's scan also contains a postscript dated 1866; that later paratext does not change the title-page date of the first couplet-text publication.

places.label: English Midlands
notes: The modern romance database assigns composition to the Midlands; this broad region should not be narrowed to a town from witness provenance or the fictional prologue's reference to Hertford.

assertion.notes: The modern database states Midlands. Furnivall cautiously describes the surviving copy's language as perhaps Midland with Northern mixture; witness dialect and work composition place are not assumed identical.
Modern English dedication, marginal explanations, preface, and apparatus belong to the edition and encoding layers, not to the abstract medieval romance's language facet.

form_statements.description: A 10,086-line narrative poem in rhyming couplets, organized in Furnivall and the XML as a 32-line prologue plus twenty-two chapters.

assertion.notes: The preceding prose contents rubric and modern editorial prose do not make the cataloged work mixed-form.
The narrative centers on knightly prowess, loyalty, warfare, courtly love, dynastic restoration, and marriage.
The prologue and older manuscript scholarship report a French source, possibly through Latin, but the exact source and direct route remain unidentified.

subject_statements.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
False accusation, delayed execution, and rash judgment

scope.description: Sirenydes's false accusation against Generides and the Persian court's judgment after Malachias's death
Generides's disguised rescue of Clarionas and Clarionas's later disguised test and healing of Generides

summaries.value: Generides, the unacknowledged son of King Aufreus of India and Sereyne of Syria, enters his father's service, resists the adulterous queen Sirenydes, and leaves for Persia after the traitor Amalek turns her accusation against him. He wins distinction in war and the love of the sultan's daughter Clarionas, defeats rivals and invading kings, helps restore Aufreus, survives further betrayals and separations, and ultimately marries Clarionas and succeeds to kingdoms in India and Persia. The romance combines dynastic restoration, courtly love, battlefield prowess, disguise and recognition, loyal friendship, and providential prediction.

A 32-line prologue on the value and transmission of old tales precedes twenty-two chapters. The chapters narrate Aufreus and Sereyne's meeting and Generides's birth; Sirenydes and Amalek's betrayal; Generides and Clarionas's courtship; wars against Bellyns and Gwynan; Clarionas's abduction and rescue; the campaigns to restore India; Amalek's death and Sirenydes's repentance; Generides's grief, disguised reunion with Clarionas, marriage, succession, and rewards to loyal companions.

This record describes only the couplet version. The rhyme-royal stanzaic version survives in Trinity College Cambridge MS O.5.2 and fragments in Emmanuel College MS 405 and early print, and it must not be counted as a second witness to this exact 10,086-line couplet text. Furnivall printed samples of that other version in the same bound volume, but CME00043 does not encode them.

The XML's two front divisions are edition and manuscript paratext: a modern dedication leaf and a prose contents rubric. The poem itself is the 10,086 L elements in the twenty-three BODY/DIV1 verse groups. Furnivall's marginal modern-English explanations and textual notes are retained at the encoding layer but are not counted as medieval verse or used to make the work multilingual or mixed-form.

extent.description: The encoded poem comprises a 32-line prologue and twenty-two numbered chapters containing 10,054 further lines, for 10,086 verse lines total in twenty-three LG elements. The count excludes the prose contents rubric, dedication, headings, and all editorial NOTE material; it agrees with the modern version-level bibliography's 10,086-line extent.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: The narrator praises the telling of old deeds and reports finding a tale associated with French rhyme and a Latin rendering at Hertford.

content_parts.label: Chapters I-XXII
content_parts.summary: The twenty-two chapter sequence narrates Generides's birth, exile, loves, wars, restorations, disguises, marriage, and succession.

related_works.title: Generides
notes: This broader romance-level identity encompasses the couplet and stanzaic Middle English versions without erasing their distinct texts and witnesses.

related_works.title: Generides (stanzaic version)
notes: The stanzaic version is an independent long English version in rhyme royal, not a second manuscript of the exact couplet text. Furnivall's volume prints fragments of it outside the CME00043 selection.

open_questions.question: What demonstrable textual relationship connects the couplet and stanzaic English versions, and what evidence survives for the prologue's reported French-to-Latin-to-English source chain?
next_steps: Compare both full English versions and the Emmanuel fragments against current source scholarship and proposed analogues.
Distinguish common narrative ancestry, direct translation, adaptation, and independent translation rather than treating shared plot as proof of one route.
Search for evidence of the reported French and Latin texts without converting a prologue claim into a surviving-source entity.

open_questions.question: How securely can the couplet version's Midlands composition be localized, and how does that claim relate to the mixed Midland and Northern features Furnivall observed in M.876?
next_steps: Consult current linguistic analysis of M.876 and distinguish authorial language from scribal copying language.
Check whether modern scholarship localizes the couplet version independently of the stanzaic version.
Do not infer composition at Hertford from the prologue's source-story or at Helmingham from later ownership.

notes: Work, version, manuscript witness, working transcript, scholarly edition, electronic encoding, repository artifact, catalog description, and facsimile are separate cataloging layers.
Morgan MS M.876 is the sole continuous witness of the couplet version; Trinity O.5.2 and Emmanuel 405 belong to the independent stanzaic version.
The 10,086-line total is both computationally reproducible from direct BODY L elements and independently reported by the current version-level bibliography.

A public seven-image manuscript selection does not support complete-codex claims; the lineage manifest records its four-image work overlap and the request route for a complete physical-object facsimile.
